title: Cat Eye Bulging and Red: Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has a bulging, red eye, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about causes, triage, and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
Understanding a Bulging, Red Eye in Cats

A bulging eye, medically referred to as exophthalmos or proptosis, occurs when the eye is pushed forward from its normal position. When accompanied by redness, it often indicates significant inflammation, increased intraocular pressure, or a physical blockage behind the globe. Common causes include glaucoma, abscesses behind the eye, severe trauma, or orbital tumors. This condition is not just a cosmetic issue; it reflects a serious underlying medical crisis.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a critical veterinary emergency. A bulging and red eye is extremely painful for your cat and carries a high risk of permanent blindness or the total loss of the eye if not treated within hours. If you notice these symptoms, do not wait for a scheduled appointment; contact the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately to prevent irreversible damage and manage your cat's pain.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are able to safely take a clear, well-lit photograph of your cat's eye, it can be an invaluable tool for the triage team. A photo allows a veterinarian to remotely assess the severity of the protrusion, the condition of the cornea, and the presence of any discharge or hemorrhage. This information helps the clinic prepare for your arrival, ensuring that the necessary specialists and equipment are ready the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Eye conditions involving bulging are considered time-sensitive emergencies. Waiting even a few hours can lead to the rupture of the eye or permanent vision loss.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure carrier to minimize movement. If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), put it on them to prevent them from pawing at or further damaging the eye.

faqs.question: Is a bulging eye always painful for a cat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even if your cat is acting stoic, the pressure and inflammation associated with a bulging eye cause significant distress and pain.
